subUnits.title: 1.1 Definition and Terms Associated with Waves
notes: Waves are rhythmic movements of water particles in the ocean or sea, caused primarily by wind action.
Key Terms Associated with Waves:
1. Wave:
• A disturbance that transfers energy through water without transferring matter
• Characterized by wavelength, wave height, wave period, and wave speed
2. Tide:
• Regular rise and fall of sea level caused by gravitational pull of Moon and Sun
• Types: Spring tides (higher high tides, lower low tides) and Neap tides (moderate tides)
3. Current:
• Continuous, directed movement of seawater
• Caused by wind, temperature differences, salinity variations, and Earth's rotation
• Examples: Gulf Stream, Canary Current, Benguela Current
4. Coast:
• Zone where land meets the sea or ocean
• Dynamic environment shaped by waves, tides, and currents
5. Swash:
• Forward movement of water up the beach after a wave breaks
• Carries sediment and deposits it on the shore
6. Backwash:
• Return flow of water down the beach after swash
• Removes sediment and transports it back to sea
7. Fetch:
• Distance over which wind blows across water surface
• Longer fetch produces larger waves
8. Wave Height:
• Vertical distance between wave crest (top) and trough (bottom)
9. Wavelength:
• Horizontal distance between two consecutive crests or troughs
10. Wave Period:
• Time taken for two consecutive waves to pass a fixed point
Wave Formation:
• Wind transfers energy to water surface
• Friction between air and water creates ripples
• Ripples grow into waves as wind continues to blow
• Wave size depends on wind speed, duration, and fetch
Wave Energy:
• Waves carry significant kinetic energy
• Energy is released when waves break on the coast
• This energy drives coastal erosion, transportation, and deposition

quiz.q: Waves are primarily caused by:
quiz.explanation: Wind blowing across water surface transfers energy, creating waves through friction.

quiz.q: The vertical distance between wave crest and trough is called:
quiz.explanation: Wave height measures the vertical distance from the highest point (crest) to lowest point (trough) of a wave.

quiz.q: Tides are mainly caused by:
opts: Gravitational pull of Moon and Sun
quiz.explanation: Tides result from gravitational forces exerted by the Moon and Sun on Earth's oceans.

quiz.q: Spring tides occur when:
opts: Moon and Sun are at right angles
quiz.explanation: Spring tides (higher high tides, lower low tides) occur during full and new moon when Moon and Sun align.

quiz.q: The forward movement of water up the beach is called:
quiz.explanation: Swash is the upward movement of water after a wave breaks, carrying sediment onto the beach.

quiz.q: Fetch refers to:
quiz.explanation: Fetch is the uninterrupted distance over which wind blows across water, affecting wave size.

quiz.q: Ocean currents are caused by all EXCEPT:
quiz.explanation: Ocean currents are driven by wind, temperature/salinity differences (thermohaline circulation), and Coriolis effect, not wave height.

quiz.q: The time taken for two consecutive waves to pass a point is:
quiz.explanation: Wave period is measured in seconds and indicates wave frequency and energy.

quiz.q: Which current flows along the west coast of Africa?
quiz.explanation: The cold Canary Current flows southward along the northwest coast of Africa, influencing climate and fishing.

quiz.q: When waves break on the coast, their energy is used for:
quiz.explanation: Wave energy drives all three coastal processes: eroding cliffs, transporting sediment, and depositing material to form beaches.

subUnits.title: 1.2 Coastal Erosion and Deposition Features
notes: Coastal Erosional Processes:
1. Hydraulic Action:
• Force of water hitting cliffs compresses air in cracks
• Repeated pressure weakens and breaks rock
2. Abrasion/Corrasion:
• Waves throw sand, pebbles, and rocks against cliffs
• Acts like sandpaper, wearing away rock surfaces
3. Attrition:
• Rocks and pebbles carried by waves collide with each other
• Become smaller, smoother, and more rounded over time
4. Solution/Corrosion:
• Chemical dissolution of soluble rocks (limestone, chalk)
• Seawater acids dissolve minerals in rock
Erosional Landforms:
1. Cliffs:
• Steep rock faces along coast formed by wave undercutting
• Retreat landward as erosion continues
2. Wave-cut Platforms:
• Gently sloping rocky surfaces at base of cliffs
• Exposed at low tide as cliff retreats
3. Caves:
• Hollows formed where waves exploit weaknesses in cliffs
• Enlarged by hydraulic action and abrasion
4. Arches:
• Formed when caves erode through headlands
• Natural rock bridges over sea
5. Stacks:
• Isolated pillars of rock left when arches collapse
• Examples: Old Harry Rocks (UK), Twelve Apostles (Australia)
6. Stumps:
• Remnants of stacks eroded to sea level
7. Bays and Headlands:
• Bays: Indented coastlines where softer rock erodes faster
• Headlands: Resistant rock projections that jut into sea
Coastal Depositional Processes:
• Occurs when wave energy decreases and sediment is dropped
• Requires: adequate sediment supply, shallow water, sheltered coast
Depositional Landforms:
1. Beaches:
• Accumulations of sand, shingle, or pebbles along shore
• Formed by swash depositing material
2. Spits:
• Long, narrow ridges of sand/shingle extending from coast into sea
• Formed by longshore drift depositing sediment
• Example: Spurn Head (UK), Lagos Bar Beach (Nigeria)
3. Bars:
• Ridges of sediment that connect two headlands or run parallel to coast
• Can form lagoons behind them
4. Tombolos:
• Bars that connect an island to the mainland
5. Sand Dunes:
• Mounds of wind-blown sand behind beaches
• Stabilized by vegetation (marram grass)
6. Mudflats and Salt Marshes:
• Low-energy environments where fine sediments accumulate
• Important for biodiversity and coastal protection
Longshore Drift:
• Movement of sediment along coast by waves approaching at an angle
• Swash carries material up beach at angle; backwash returns it straight down
• Results in net movement of sediment along coastline
Nigerian Coastal Examples:
• Lagos coastline: beaches, spits, bars affected by erosion
• Niger Delta: extensive mudflats, mangroves, depositional environment
• Calabar coast: cliffs, wave-cut platforms in some areas

quiz.q: The process where waves compress air in rock cracks is called:
quiz.explanation: Hydraulic action involves the force of water and compressed air breaking rock apart in cracks and joints.

quiz.q: Attrition refers to:
quiz.explanation: Attrition is the wearing down of rocks and pebbles as they collide with each other in wave action.

quiz.q: A wave-cut platform is found:
opts: At the base of retreating cliffs
quiz.explanation: Wave-cut platforms are gently sloping rocky surfaces exposed at low tide as cliffs erode and retreat.

quiz.q: Stacks are formed from the collapse of:
quiz.explanation: When a coastal arch collapses due to continued erosion, the isolated pillar remaining is called a stack.

quiz.q: Longshore drift moves sediment:
quiz.explanation: Longshore drift transports sediment parallel to the coast due to waves approaching at an angle.

quiz.q: A spit is formed by:
quiz.explanation: Spits are elongated deposits of sand/shingle extending from coast, formed by longshore drift.

quiz.q: Which feature connects an island to the mainland?
quiz.explanation: A tombolo is a bar or spit that grows to connect an island with the mainland or another island.

quiz.q: Solution as an erosional process affects mainly:
quiz.explanation: Solution (corrosion) chemically dissolves soluble rocks like limestone and chalk in seawater.

quiz.q: Bays form where:
opts: Soft rock erodes faster than hard rock
quiz.explanation: Bays develop where softer, less resistant rocks erode more rapidly than adjacent harder rocks, creating indentations.

quiz.q: Mudflats and salt marshes are important because they:
opts: Support biodiversity and protect coasts from erosion
quiz.explanation: Mudflats and salt marshes provide habitats for wildlife, filter pollutants, and buffer coasts against storm surges.
